title: Detailed Dive: Variance Formulas and lambda-star
text: labeled size nn unlabeled size NN: Estimator θ̂λ=Y‾L+λ(f‾U−f‾L). \hat\theta_\lambda = \bar{Y}_L + \lambda(\bar{f}_U - \bar{f}_L). Variance Var⁡(θ̂λ)=σY2n+λ2σf2(1N+1n)−2λCov⁡(Y,f)n. \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_\lambda) = \frac{\sigma_Y^2}{n}  + \lambda^2 \sigma_f^2 \left(\frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{n}\right)  - \frac{2\lambda\,\operatorname{Cov}(Y,f)}{n}. Minimizing yields λ*=Cov⁡(Y,f)(1+n/N)σf2,Var⁡(θ̂λ*)=σY2n−Cov⁡(Y,f)2σf2Nn(n+N). \lambda^* = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(Y,f)}{(1 + n/N)\sigma_f^2}, \qquad \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\lambda^*}) = \frac{\sigma_Y^2}{n} - \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(Y,f)^2}{\sigma_f^2}\frac{N}{n(n+N)}.
code: lambda_opt <- function(n, N, cov_y_f, var_f) cov_y_f / ((1 + n / N) * var_f) lambda_opt(n = 200, N = 5000, cov_y_f = 0.6, var_f = 0.6) #> [1] 0.9615385

title: Detailed Dive: Variance Formulas and lambda-star
text: Given effect size Δ\Delta, target power 1−β1-\beta, level α\alpha, |Δ|Var⁡(θ̂λ*)≥z1−α/2+z1−β. \frac{\lvert\Delta\rvert}{\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta_{\lambda^*})}} \ge z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}. power_ppi_mean() solves directly; use instead hand algebra:
code: power_ppi_mean(   delta      = 0.2,   N          = 5000,   n          = NULL,   power      = 0.9,   sigma_y2   = 1.0,   sigma_f2   = 0.6,   cov_y_f    = 0.6,   lambda_mode = "oracle" ) #> [1] 109

title: Detailed Dive: Variance Formulas and lambda-star
text: sensitivity/specificity (sens,spec)(\text{sens}, \text{spec}) prevalence pp, pŶ=sensp+(1−spec)(1−p),Cov⁡(Y,f)=p(1−p)(sens+spec−1). p_{\hat{Y}} = \text{sens}\,p + (1-\text{spec})(1-p), \qquad \operatorname{Cov}(Y,f) = p(1-p)(\text{sens} + \text{spec} - 1).  helper binary_moments_from_sens_spec() returns Var⁡(Y)\operatorname{Var}(Y), Var⁡(f)\operatorname{Var}(f), Cov⁡(Y,f)\operatorname{Cov}(Y,f): Use outputs directly power_ppi_mean().

title: Detailed Dive: Sample Size & Metrics Interface
text: now demonstrate regression contrast mode. Generate Data: Construct PPI++ blocks sandwich variance estimators: Solve sample size contrast c⊤βc^\top \beta:
code: p    <- 4 n_l  <- 400 n_u  <- 3000 beta <- c(0.7, -0.5, 0.2, 1.0)  X_l <- matrix(rnorm(n_l*p), n_l, p) X_u <- matrix(rnorm(n_u*p), n_u, p)  Y_l <- drop(X_l %*% beta + rnorm(n_l, sd = 0.7)) f_l <- drop(X_l %*% beta + rnorm(n_l, sd = 0.6)) f_u <- drop(X_u %*% beta + rnorm(n_u, sd = 0.6)) blocks <- compute_ppi_blocks(   model_type = "ols",   X_l = X_l, Y_l = Y_l, f_l = f_l,   X_u = X_u, f_u = f_u,   beta = beta ) c_vec <- c(0, 1, 0, 0) delta <- as.numeric(t(c_vec) %*% beta)  power_ppi_regression(   delta       = delta,   N           = n_u,   n           = NULL,   power       = 0.90,   lambda_mode = "vanilla",   c           = c_vec,   H_L         = blocks$H_L,   H_U         = blocks$H_U,   Sigma_YY    = blocks$Sigma_YY,   Sigma_ff_l  = blocks$Sigma_ff_l,   Sigma_ff_u  = blocks$Sigma_ff_u,   Sigma_Yf    = blocks$Sigma_Yf ) #> [1] 35  power_ppi_regression(   delta       = delta,   N           = n_u,   n           = NULL,   power       = 0.90,   lambda_mode = "oracle",   c           = c_vec,   H_L         = blocks$H_L,   H_U         = blocks$H_U,   Sigma_YY    = blocks$Sigma_YY,   Sigma_ff_l  = blocks$Sigma_ff_l,   Sigma_ff_u  = blocks$Sigma_ff_u,   Sigma_Yf    = blocks$Sigma_Yf ) #> [1] 23

what: Regression-Mode Sample Size — GLM: Logistic Regression
title: Detailed Dive: Sample Size & Metrics Interface
text: (follow similar steps described OLS case ) Construct PPI++ blocks sandwich variance estimators similar previous OLS case: solve sample size:
code: p    <- 3 n_l  <- 500 n_u  <- 2500 beta <- c(1.2, -0.6, 0.5)  X_l <- matrix(rnorm(n_l*p), n_l, p) X_u <- matrix(rnorm(n_u*p), n_u, p)  eta_l <- drop(X_l %*% beta) eta_u <- drop(X_u %*% beta)  prob_l <- plogis(eta_l) prob_u <- plogis(eta_u)  Y_l <- rbinom(n_l, 1, prob_l)  f_l <- plogis(eta_l + rnorm(n_l, sd = 1.5)) f_u <- plogis(eta_u + rnorm(n_u, sd = 1.5)) blocks_glm <- compute_ppi_blocks(   model_type = "glm",   family     = "binomial",   X_l = X_l, Y_l = Y_l, f_l = f_l,   X_u = X_u, f_u = f_u,   beta = beta ) c_vec <- c(1, 0, 0) delta <- as.numeric(t(c_vec) %*% beta)  power_ppi_regression(   delta       = delta,   N           = n_u,   n           = NULL,   power       = 0.80,   lambda_mode = "vanilla",   c           = c_vec,   H_L         = blocks_glm$H_L,   H_U         = blocks_glm$H_U,   Sigma_YY    = blocks_glm$Sigma_YY,   Sigma_ff_l  = blocks_glm$Sigma_ff_l,   Sigma_ff_u  = blocks_glm$Sigma_ff_u,   Sigma_Yf    = blocks_glm$Sigma_Yf ) #> [1] 60  power_ppi_regression(   delta       = delta,   N           = n_u,   n           = NULL,   power       = 0.80,   lambda_mode = "oracle",   c           = c_vec,   H_L         = blocks_glm$H_L,   H_U         = blocks_glm$H_U,   Sigma_YY    = blocks_glm$Sigma_YY,   Sigma_ff_l  = blocks_glm$Sigma_ff_l,   Sigma_ff_u  = blocks_glm$Sigma_ff_u,   Sigma_Yf    = blocks_glm$Sigma_Yf ) #> [1] 41

title: Detailed Dive: Sample Size & Metrics Interface
text: chunk visualizes required (nn) decreases effect size grows, oracle λ\lambda PPI++ estimator provides clear reduction sample size estimations vanilla PPI:
code: delta_grid <- seq(0.10, 0.40, length.out = 40)  ## Shared parameters (mean-estimation case) N       <- 3000 sy2     <- 1.0      # Var(Y) sf2     <- 0.4      # Var(f) cyf     <- 0.15     # Cov(Y, f) lambda_user_value <- 0.70   # example user-specified λ  ## Compute required n for each λ mode results <- lapply(delta_grid, function(d) {   data.frame(     delta = d,     vanilla = power_ppi_mean(       delta       = d,       N           = N,       n           = NULL,       power       = 0.80,       lambda_mode = "vanilla",       sigma_y2    = sy2,       sigma_f2    = sf2,       cov_y_f     = cyf     ),     oracle = power_ppi_mean(       delta       = d,       N           = N,       n           = NULL,       power       = 0.80,       lambda_mode = "oracle",       sigma_y2    = sy2,       sigma_f2    = sf2,       cov_y_f     = cyf     ),     user = power_ppi_mean(       delta       = d,       N           = N,       n           = NULL,       power       = 0.80,       lambda_mode = "user",       lambda_user = lambda_user_value,       sigma_y2    = sy2,       sigma_f2    = sf2,       cov_y_f     = cyf     )   ) })  df <- bind_rows(results) |>   pivot_longer(cols = c("vanilla", "oracle", "user"),                names_to = "mode",                values_to = "n")  ggplot(df, aes(delta, n, color = mode)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1.8) +   scale_color_manual(     values = c(       vanilla = "#1B9E77",       oracle  = "#D95F02",       user    = "#7570B3"     ),     labels = c(       vanilla = "PPI (vanilla λ = 1)",       oracle  = "PPI++ (oracle λ*)",       user    = paste0("User λ = ", lambda_user_value)     )   ) +   labs(     title = "Required Labeled Sample Size vs. Effect Size δ",     subtitle = paste("N =", N, " unlabeled samples"),     y = "Required labeled sample size n",     x = "Effect size δ",     color = "λ-mode"   ) +   theme_pppower(base_size = 12)

title: Power Calculations for Prediction-Powered Inference
text: Many modern studies access : Labeled data (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i) =1,…,ni = 1, \ldots, n — expensive obtain (human annotations, lab assays, expert reviews) Unlabeled data X̃j\tilde{X}_j j=1,…,Nj = 1, \ldots, N — cheap abundant, ML predictions f(X̃j)f(\tilde{X}_j) available PPI combines sources valid, efficient inference. PPI++ estimator population mean θ*=E[Y]\theta^* = E[Y] : θ̂λ=Y‾L+λ(f‾U−f‾L)\hat{\theta}_\lambda = \bar{Y}_L + \lambda(\bar{f}_U - \bar{f}_L) λ\lambda controls blend labeled data predictions. key insight: good predictions reduce amount labeled data needed given level statistical power.

title: Power Calculations for Prediction-Powered Inference
text: PPI++ variance oracle λ*\lambda^* : Var(θ̂λ*)=σY2n−Cov(Y,f)2σf2⋅Nn(n+N)\text{Var}(\hat{\theta}_{\lambda^*}) = \frac{\sigma_Y^2}{n} - \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,f)^2}{\sigma_f^2} \cdot \frac{N}{n(n+N)} Solving required labeled sample size (quadratic nn): n≥σY2−S2N+(σY2−S2N)2+4S2NσY2(1−ρYf2)2S2n \geq \frac{\sigma_Y^2 - S^2 N + \sqrt{(\sigma_Y^2 - S^2 N)^2 + 4 S^2 N \sigma_Y^2 (1 - \rho_{Yf}^2)}}{2S^2} S2=(Δ/(z1−α/2+z1−β))2S^2 = (\Delta / (z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}))^2. Rule thumb: sample size ratio satisfies n𝙿𝙿𝙸++/nclassical≈1−R2n_{\texttt{PPI++}} / n_{\text{classical}} \approx 1 - R^2, R2R^2 squared correlation YY f(X)f(X). predictor explaining 80% variance cuts labeled data needs ~80%.

title: Power Calculations for Prediction-Powered Inference
text: three-step workflow: compute power → solve required nn → verify Monte Carlo.
code: library(pppower)  # Step 1: Compute power for a given sample size power_ppi_mean(   delta    = 0.2,   N        = 5000,   n        = 200,   var_f    = 0.6,   var_res  = 0.4,   cov_y_f  = 0.6,   alpha    = 0.05 )  # Step 2: Solve for required labeled n to achieve 80% power power_ppi_mean(   delta       = 0.2,   N           = 5000,   n           = NULL,   power       = 0.80,   sigma_y2    = 1.0,   sigma_f2    = 0.49,   cov_y_f     = 0.63,   lambda_mode = "oracle" )  # Step 3: Verify with Monte Carlo simulation simulate_ppi_mean(   R     = 2000,   n     = 100,   N     = 5000,   delta = 0.2,   var_f   = 0.49,   var_res = 0.51,   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Power Calculations for Prediction-Powered Inference
text: pppower supports three ways specify variance components: 1. Direct moments — supply σY2\sigma_Y^2, σf2\sigma_f^2, Cov(Y,f)\text{Cov}(Y, f) directly: 2. Prediction variance / residual variance — decompose outcome variance via var_f var_res: 3. Metrics interface — supply sensitivity/specificity (binary) MSE/R2R^2 (continuous):

title: Construct Hessian/Fisher and Covariance Blocks for PPI/PPI++ Regression — compute_ppi_blocks
text: model_type Character string: "ols" "glm". Determines whether estimator linear regression (OLS) GLM. X_l Matrix labeled covariates (n x p). Y_l Vector labeled responses (required OLS GLM). f_l Vector model predictions labeled data. X_u Matrix unlabeled covariates (N x p). f_u Vector model predictions unlabeled data. beta Numeric vector regression coefficients used Hessian/Fisher evaluation. Required GLM; optional OLS. family GLM family ("binomial" "gaussian"). used model_type = "glm".

title: Construct Hessian/Fisher and Covariance Blocks for PPI/PPI++ Regression — compute_ppi_blocks

title: Construct Hessian/Fisher and Covariance Blocks for PPI/PPI++ Regression — compute_ppi_blocks
code: set.seed(1) p <- 3 n_l <- 400 n_u <- 2000  X_l <- matrix(rnorm(n_l * p), n_l, p) X_u <- matrix(rnorm(n_u * p), n_u, p) beta <- c(1, -0.5, 0.3)  # Labeled response and predictions Y_l <- drop(X_l %*% beta + rnorm(n_l)) f_l <- drop(X_l %*% beta + rnorm(n_l, sd = 0.3)) f_u <- drop(X_u %*% beta + rnorm(n_u, sd = 0.3))  blocks <- compute_ppi_blocks(   model_type = "ols",   X_l = X_l, Y_l = Y_l, f_l = f_l,   X_u = X_u, f_u = f_u,   beta = beta )  # Use in sample size solver: c_vec <- c(1, 0, 0) delta <- as.numeric(t(c_vec) %*% beta)  power_ppi_regression(   delta = delta, N = n_u, power = 0.80,   lambda_mode = "oracle",   c = c_vec,   H_L = blocks$H_L, H_U = blocks$H_U,   Sigma_YY = blocks$Sigma_YY,   Sigma_ff_l = blocks$Sigma_ff_l,   Sigma_ff_u = blocks$Sigma_ff_u,   Sigma_Yf = blocks$Sigma_Yf ) #> [1] 8

what: Type I error and power curve for the PPI mean estimator — type1_error_curve_mean_dgp
title: Type I error and power curve for the PPI mean estimator — type1_error_curve_mean_dgp
text: Computes empirical analytical Type error (power) across grid effect sizes prediction-powered mean estimator, without simulating superpopulation. Instead generating data, supply key population quantities directly: \(\sigma_f^2 = Var(f(X))\) \(\sigma_{\mathrm{res}}^2 = Var(Y - f(X))\). Analytical power computed normal theory formula \(\sqrt{\sigma_f^2/N + \sigma_{\mathrm{res}}^2/n}\), empirical power estimated via repeated Monte Carlo simulations using simulate_ppi_vanilla_mean().
